text: Math Pattern: GCD and LCM. GCD via Euclidean algorithm: gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a%b), base case gcd(a,0)=a. Time: O(log(min(a,b))). LCM(a,b) = a*b // gcd(a,b). Use for: fraction simplification, finding common periods, minimum common multiples. Extended Euclidean: finds x,y such that ax+by=gcd(a,b), used in modular inverse.

Math Pattern: Prime factorization. Trial division: for i from 2 to sqrt(n): while n%i==0: factors.append(i); n//=i; if n>1: factors.append(n). Time: O(sqrt(n)). For multiple queries: Sieve of Eratosthenes precomputes smallest prime factor (SPF) for each number, factorize in O(log n) per number. Time: O(n log log n) sieve.

Math Pattern: Sieve of Eratosthenes. Find all primes up to n. Initialize is_prime[0..n]=True. For each prime p starting from 2, mark multiples p², p²+p, ... as composite. Time: O(n log log n), Space: O(n). Optimizations: start marking from p², skip even numbers. For large ranges: segmented sieve uses O(sqrt(n)) space.

Math Pattern: Modular arithmetic. (a+b)%m = ((a%m)+(b%m))%m. (a*b)%m = ((a%m)*(b%m))%m. Subtraction: (a-b)%m = ((a%m)-(b%m)+m)%m. Division: requires modular inverse. Modular inverse: a^(m-2) mod m (when m is prime, Fermat's little theorem). Or use extended Euclidean. Always reduce intermediate results to avoid overflow.

Math Pattern: Fast exponentiation (binary exponentiation). Compute a^b mod m in O(log b) time. Template: result=1, base=a%m; while b>0: if b&1: result=result*base%m; base=base*base%m; b>>=1. Use for: large power computations, RSA, modular inverse (Fermat's little theorem). Space: O(1). Recursive: a^b = (a^(b//2))^2 * a^(b%2).

Math Pattern: Combinatorics (nCr mod p). Precompute factorials and inverse factorials. fact[i] = i! mod p. inv_fact[i] = modular_inverse(fact[i]). nCr = fact[n] * inv_fact[r] * inv_fact[n-r] % p. Time: O(n) precomputation, O(1) per query. For p not prime or large n: Lucas' theorem or Andrew Granville's method.

Math Pattern: Number of divisors. For n = p1^a1 * p2^a2 * ... * pk^ak, number of divisors = (a1+1)*(a2+1)*...*(ak+1). Sum of divisors uses similar formula. For counting divisors of all numbers 1..n: O(n log n) approach — for each d, add 1 to all multiples. Use for: perfect numbers, abundant numbers, divisor functions in competitive programming.

Math Pattern: Euclidean geometry basics. Distance: sqrt((x2-x1)²+(y2-y1)²). To avoid sqrt: compare squared distances. Cross product (a×b): ax*by - ay*bx. Positive: counter-clockwise, Negative: clockwise, Zero: collinear. Use cross product for: orientation test, convex hull, checking if point is left/right of line. Dot product: ax*bx + ay*by = |a||b|cos(θ).

Math Pattern: Matrix exponentiation. Compute linear recurrences in O(k³ log n) instead of O(kn). Express recurrence as matrix multiplication: F(n) = M^n * F(0). Use fast matrix exponentiation. Use for: Fibonacci in O(log n), counting paths of length n in graph, nth term of linear recurrence. Template: matrix_pow(M, n, mod).

Math Pattern: Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT). Solve system: x ≡ a1 (mod m1), x ≡ a2 (mod m2), ... when moduli are pairwise coprime. Unique solution mod M=m1*m2*...*mk. x = sum(ai * Mi * yi) mod M where Mi = M/mi, yi = modular_inverse(Mi, mi). Use for: combining remainders, calendar calculations, RSA.

Math Pattern: Prefix sum / difference array. Prefix sum: ps[i] = sum(arr[0..i-1]). Range sum query [l,r] = ps[r+1] - ps[l]. Time: O(n) build, O(1) query. Difference array: for range updates [l,r] += v, do diff[l]+=v, diff[r+1]-=v, then prefix sum to recover. 2D prefix sum for rectangle queries. Time: O(m*n) build, O(1) query.

Math Pattern: Catalan numbers. Cn = C(2n,n)/(n+1). C0=1, C1=1, C2=2, C3=5, C4=14. Count: valid parenthesizations, binary trees with n+1 leaves, paths not crossing diagonal, polygon triangulations. Recurrence: Cn = sum(Ci * C(n-1-i)) for i in [0,n-1]. Time: O(n²) DP or O(n) using formula. Use when counting recursive structures.

Math Pattern: Pigeonhole principle. If n+1 items are placed in n containers, at least one container has 2+ items. Use to prove existence without finding: maximum repeated element, longest increasing subsequence bound, birthday paradox, hash collision. In algorithms: proves greedy works, establishes lower bounds, cycle detection in functional graphs.

Math Pattern: Pascal's triangle and binomial theorem. (a+b)^n = sum(C(n,k) * a^k * b^(n-k)). Build Pascal's triangle: C[i][j] = C[i-1][j-1] + C[i-1][j]. Time: O(n²). Properties: sum of row = 2^n, alternating sum = 0. Use for: probability calculations, counting paths, expanding polynomials, combinatorial identities in DP.

Math Pattern: Number theory — Euler's totient function. φ(n) = count of integers in [1,n] coprime to n. For prime p: φ(p)=p-1. For prime power: φ(p^k)=p^k - p^(k-1). Multiplicative: φ(mn)=φ(m)φ(n) when gcd(m,n)=1. Compute from factorization: φ(n) = n * product(1-1/p) for prime p dividing n. Euler's theorem: a^φ(n) ≡ 1 (mod n) when gcd(a,n)=1.

Math Pattern: Inclusion-exclusion principle. |A∪B∪C| = |A|+|B|+|C| - |A∩B| - |A∩C| - |B∩C| + |A∩B∩C|. General: alternating sum of intersections of subsets. Use for: counting integers divisible by at least one of several primes, derangements, surjective functions. Time: O(2^k) for k sets. Crucial in: probability, combinatorics, DP state corrections.

Math Pattern: Geometric series and logarithms. Sum of geometric series: a + ar + ar² + ... + ar^(n-1) = a*(r^n - 1)/(r-1). Infinite series (|r|<1): a/(1-r). Log identities: log(ab)=log(a)+log(b), log(a^k)=k*log(a). Use logs for: comparing products without overflow (compare sum of logs), detecting patterns in recurrences, analyzing algorithm complexity.

Math Pattern: Floating point precision handling. Never compare floats with ==; use abs(a-b) < epsilon (e.g., 1e-9). For geometry: avoid division when possible, use cross products. Sort by angle using atan2 carefully. Integer arithmetic is exact; convert to integers when possible (multiply coordinates by scale factor). Binary search on floats uses fixed iterations (100) or epsilon convergence.

Math Pattern: Bit counting (Hamming weight) applications. popcount(x^y) = Hamming distance between x and y. Sum of all XOR pairs = 32 * sum(bit_i_contribution). For each bit position, count numbers with bit set (cnt), contribution = cnt * (n-cnt) * 2. Total distance = sum across all bits. Use in: closest pair by Hamming distance, error correction codes, similarity metrics.

Math Pattern: Random algorithms and expected value. Expected value E[X] = sum(x * P(X=x)). Linearity of expectation: E[X+Y] = E[X]+E[Y] always (even when dependent). Use for: expected number of collisions in hashing, coupon collector problem (expected n*H(n) draws), quick sort expected O(n log n). Randomization often simplifies hard problems: random pivot, random hashing.
